instruction: Rewrite the content to meet insurance regulatory and compliance standards.

input.draft_text:
Types Of Bonuses In Life Insurance
Some life insurance plans can offer additional bonus which can help you create a good corpus for your financial goals. Understanding the types of bonuses in life insurance helps policyholders know how their plan can enhance long-term value. Bonuses are additional benefits declared by insurers, usually from surplus profits, and are added to eligible life insurance policies. Common types include reversionary bonuses, which are added to the policy corpus, and terminal bonuses, which are paid at maturity or claim settlement. These bonuses can potentially increase the returns offered by a life insurance plan.
What is Bonus in Life Insurance?
Bonus in life insurance policy is an additional benefit that a policyholder can earn from a participating life insurance policy.
A bonus in life insurance is an additional benefit that an insurer may declare from its surplus profits. It is usually allocated to participating policies and added to the policyholder’s corpus. Bonuses can enhance the maturity value or death benefit, thereby offering enhanced benefits for your goals.
Which Life Insurance Policies are Eligible for Bonuses?
Not all plans provide a bonus in life insurance. Bonuses are usually offered under participating (with-profit) life insurance policies. These policies share a part of the insurer’s surplus with policyholders. Eligibility depends on regular premium payment and policy terms.
Policies that may qualify for bonuses:
Participating whole life plans
Participating endowment policies
Participating money-back plans
Types of Bonuses under Life Insurance
Before describing the various types of bonuses, such as the terminal bonus in life insurance, an interim bonus, cash bonuses, and so on, let us first understand how a bonus may work in the context of life insurance.
Firstly, one should know that bonuses are paid under a participating life insurance policy. The difference between a non-participating policy and a participating policy is that, in the latter, the policy may accrue bonuses based on the profits earned by the insurance company. The term ‘participating life insurance policy’ indicates that the policyholder may participate in the profits of the insurance company, as defined and limited by the policy wordings[1].
Details regarding the participating element of the life insurance policy may be found in the policy documents, which the buyer can benefit by reading thoroughly.
Here are the types of bonuses in life insurance you should know about:
• Simple Reversionary Bonus [1]
This bonus is calculated after taking a certain percentage of the sum assured into consideration. The simple reversionary bonus is usually declared on an annual basis and may be added to the current value of the policy. However, the bonus may be paid out only during the maturity of the policy or when the policyholder passes away, minus the standard deductions mentioned under the policy.
Let’s look at the following illustration to understand this better:
Mr Arjun has a participating life insurance policy with a sum assured of Rs 75 lakhs. As per his policy, he receives a simple reversionary bonus at the rate of 3% per year. Thus, every year, a bonus of Rs 22,500 will be accrued under his policy.
*Figures are for illustrative purposes only
• Compound Reversionary Bonus [1]
A Compound Reversionary Bonus is calculated on the sum assured plus bonuses already added in previous years. This means each year’s bonus compounds, steadily increasing the policy’s value and overall corpus over time. It is one of the important types of bonuses in life insurance, usually paid at maturity or during a death claim after standard deductions.
Example: If the sum assured is ₹1,00,000 and a 5% bonus is declared, the first-year bonus is ₹5,000. Next year, the bonus is calculated on ₹1,05,000, giving ₹5,250 at 5% bonus.Figures are for illustrative purposes only
• Terminal Bonus [1]
The one-time terminal bonus in life insurance is paid out when the policyholder completes a pre-determined policy term or when a death claim is raised. It may also be referred to as the persistency bonus and may be rewarded at the discretion of the insurer.
• Cash Bonus [1]
Unlike the previously mentioned types of life insurance bonuses, a cash bonus may be paid out to the policyholder year-on-year, minus the standard deductions, rather than accumulated till maturity.
For instance, if the annual premium of your life insurance is Rs 30,000 and the cash bonus rate declared is 5%, then you may receive Rs 1500 as the cash bonus for the year.
*Figures are for illustrative purposes only
• Interim Bonus [1]
In case there is a claim towards death or maturity benefit in midst of a year, that is, between two bonus declaration dates, the insurer pays the interim bonus on a pro-rata basis to the policyholder. The interim bonus is computed basis the interim bonus rates declared by the insurance company, for the short period in between the bonus declaration date and the maturity/claim date for which the policy has not received bonus.
For example, Mr. Rajesh’s life insurance policy is maturing in Dec’2023. He would have earned his last bonus at the end of the financial year 2022-23. However, what about the bonus for the interim period between April,2023 to December,2023. In such a scenario, if a claim is raised between two bonus declaration dates, the insurer will compute an interim bonus for the period from April,2023 to December,2023.
Key Benefits of Life Insurance Bonuses
Enhances maturity/death benefitA bonus in life insurance is added to the sum assured, significantly increasing the maturity value or death benefit. This ensures policyholders or their families receive a higher payout from their life insurance policies.
Provides additional returns without extra premiumPolicyholders gain added value through bonuses without paying extra premiums, which strengthens their overall corpus. This feature makes participating policies appealing, as the bonus works like additional returns while keeping regular contributions unchanged throughout the policy tenure.
Motivates policyholders to stay investedBonuses encourage policyholders to continue paying premiums and maintaining their policy regularly over time. Staying invested ensures consistent growth of the corpus, maximises financial benefits, and promotes disciplined planning for long-term security and financial independence.
How Are Bonuses Calculated under Life Insurance?
A bonus in life insurance is generally associated with participating or with-profit policies. These plans have a savings element, which means the insurer may declare bonuses from surplus profits earned during a financial year. The way bonuses are calculated depends on a variety of factors that differ from one policy to another.
One of the primary factors is the performance of the underlying assets where premiums are invested. If the insurer generates high returns, the likelihood of declaring higher bonuses improves. The type of bonus also matters, since reversionary bonuses are added to the sum assured while terminal bonuses are paid only at maturity or claim settlement. In certain cases, such as compound reversionary bonuses, previously declared bonuses are added to the base sum assured, which further increases the amount of future bonuses.
Regular premium payment is equally important because only active policies remain eligible for bonuses. Policyholders should also carefully review their plan’s terms and conditions to understand the eligibility criteria. Consulting an insurer’s representative or using a bonus calculator can help estimate the potential corpus growth from bonuses.
Key Takeaways
A bonus in life insurance is declared on participating policies from the surplus profits that the insurer earns.
Common types include reversionary and terminal bonuses, which can enhance the corpus.
Paying the premium regularly is essential for bonus eligibility.
Bonus amounts may depend on policy terms, asset performance, and the surplus earned by the insurer.
You can check the policy documents to understand the types of bonuses your policy might be eligible for.
Conclusion
Understand how a life insurance policy bonus works. The bonus can add to your policy benefits and help you plan for your financial goals effectively. While the bonus rates may vary, timely payment of premiums and life insurance policy renewals are key for eligibility. However, before you decide, review each step of buying life insurance, examine your future goals, and choose a suitable plan offering bonus additions that match your needs.
FAQs
1. Does the premium increase if a bonus has been declared the previous year?
Your premium is unlikely to be affected by the bonuses declared by the insurance company. Other factors, such as an increase in age, change in lifestyle and health issues, may have an impact on the premium. One can use the life insurance premium calculator to get an estimate of their premium amount based on different factors.
2. Are bonuses declared every year?
Whether bonuses are declared every year or not may depend on multiple factors like type of plan, insurance company’s performance, market performance, etc. It may prove to be helpful to consider the consistency of bonuses and the rate of bonuses when choosing an insurance company or policy. One should also keep in mind that past performance in terms of bonuses declared by life insurance companies may not be indicative of future bonus declaration.
3. What is the difference between a simple reversionary bonus and a compound reversionary bonus?A simple bonus applies only to the sum assured, while a compound bonus includes previously declared amounts, potentially increasing the overall policy benefits over time.
4. Are life insurance bonuses guaranteed for policyholders?No, bonuses are not guaranteed. They depend on insurer surplus, policy terms, and consistent premium payment. Regular renewal of life insurance improves eligibility for bonuses when declared.
5. Can I withdraw the bonus before maturity?Generally, bonuses are payable at maturity or claim settlement. They cannot be withdrawn early. However, under policies offering cash bonus, the bonus earned can be paid to the policyholder in the same policy year. Such bonuses can be withdrawn before maturity.
6. Do all insurers declare the same type of bonuses?No, bonus types vary by insurer and plan. The bonus rates depend on the surplus that the insurer earns.
Disclaimer:
The views stated in this article are not to be construed as investment advice and readers are suggested to seek independent financial advice before making any investment decisions. For more details on risk factors, terms and conditions please read the sales brochure & policy document (available on www.bajajallianzlife.com) carefully before concluding a sale. output.final_text:
Types Of Bonuses in Life Insurance
Some life insurance plans offer additional bonus (if declared) which can help you enhance the corpus for your financial goals. Understanding the types of bonuses in life insurance helps policyholders know how their plan can enhance long-term value. Bonuses are additional benefits which may be declared by insurers, usually from surplus profits, and are added to eligible life insurance policies. Common types include reversionary bonuses, which are added to the policy corpus, and terminal bonuses, which are paid at maturity or claim settlement. These bonuses can
potentially increase the returns offered by a life insurance plan.
What is Bonus in Life Insurance?
Bonus (if declared) in life insurance policy is an additional benefit that a policyholder may earn from a participating life insurance policy.
A bonus in life insurance is an additional benefit that a life insurer may declare from its surplus profits. It is usually allocated to participating policies and added to the policyholder’s corpus. Bonuses (if declared) can enhance the maturity value or death benefit, thereby offering enhanced benefits for your goals .
Which Life Insurance Policies are Eligible for Bonuses?
Not all plans provide a bonus in life insurance. Bonuses (if declared) are usually offered under participating (with-profit) life insurance policies. In such policies, the life insurer distributes a part of its profit to eligible policyholders in the form of bonuses. Eligibility depends on regular premium payment and policy terms.
Plans that qualify for bonuses (if declared):
● Participating whole life plans
● Participating endowment plans
● Participating money-back plans
Types of Bonuses under Life Insurance
Before describing the various types of bonuses, such as the terminal bonus in life insurance, an interim bonus, cash bonuses, and so on, let us first understand how a bonus may work in the context of life insurance.
Firstly, one should know that bonuses are paid under a participating life insurance policy. The difference between a non-participating policy and a participating policy is that, in the latter, the policy may accrue bonuses based on the profits earned by the insurance company. The term ‘participating life insurance policy’ indicates that the policyholder may participate in the profits of the insurance company, as defined and limited by the policy wordings[1].
Details regarding the participating element of the life insurance policy may be found in the policy documents, which the buyer can benefit by reading thoroughly.
Here are the types of bonuses in life insurance you should know about:
• Simple Reversionary Bonus [1]
This bonus is calculated after taking a certain percentage of the sum assured into consideration. The simple reversionary bonus is usually declared on an annual basis and may be added to the current value of the policy. However, the bonus may be paid out only during the maturity of the policy or when the policyholder passes away, minus the standard deductions mentioned under the policy.
Let’s look at the following illustration to understand this better:
Mr Arjun has a participating life insurance policy with a sum assured of Rs 75 lakhs. As per his policy, he receives a simple reversionary bonus at the rate of 3% per year. Thus, every year, a bonus of Rs 22,500 will be accrued under his policy.
*Figures are for illustrative purposes only
• Compound Reversionary Bonus [1]
A Compound Reversionary Bonus is calculated on the sum assured plus bonuses already added in previous years. This means each year’s bonus compounds, steadily increasing the overall corpus over time. It is one of the important types of bonuses in life insurance, usually paid at maturity or during a death claim after standard deductions.
Example: If the sum assured is ₹1,00,000 and a 5% bonus is declared, the first-year bonus is ₹5,000. Next year, the bonus is calculated on ₹1,05,000, giving ₹5,250 at 5% bonus. Figures are for illustrative purposes only
• Terminal Bonus [1]
The one-time terminal bonus in life insurance is paid out when the policyholder completes a pre- determined policy term or when a death claim is raised. It may also be referred to as the persistency bonus and may be rewarded at the discretion of the insurer.
• Cash Bonus [1]
Unlike the previously mentioned types of life insurance bonuses, a cash bonus may be paid out to the policyholder year-on-year, minus the standard deductions, rather than accumulated till maturity.
For instance, if the annual premium of your life insurance is Rs 30,000 and the cash bonus rate declared is 5%, then you may receive Rs 1500 as the cash bonus for the year.
*Figures are for illustrative purposes only
• Interim Bonus [1]
In case there is a claim towards death or maturity benefit in midst of a year, that is, between two bonus declaration dates, the insurer pays the interim bonus on a pro-rata basis to the policyholder. The interim bonus is computed basis the interim bonus rates declared by the insurance company, for
the short period in between the bonus declaration date and the maturity/claim date for which the policy has not received bonus.
For example, Mr. Rajesh’s life insurance policy is maturing in Dec’2023. He would have earned his last bonus at the end of the financial year 2022-23. However, what about the bonus for the interim period between April,2023 to December,2023. In such a scenario, if a claim is raised between two bonus declaration dates, the insurer will compute an interim bonus for the period from April,2023 to December,2023.
Key Benefits of Life Insurance Bonuses (if declared)
Enhances maturity/death benefit A bonus (if declared) in life insurance is added to the sum assured, potentially increasing the maturity value or death benefit. This ensures policyholders or their families receive a higher payout from their life insurance policies.
Provides added value Policyholders gain added value through bonuses (if declared), which strengthens their overall corpus. This feature makes participating policies appealing, as the bonus (if declared) works like additional returns while keeping regular contributions unchanged throughout the policy tenure.
Motivates policyholders to stay invested Bonuses (if declared) encourage policyholders to continue paying premiums and maintaining their policy regularly over time. Staying invested ensures consistent growth of the corpus, maximises financial benefits, and promotes disciplined planning for long-term security and financial independence.
How Are Bonuses Calculated under Life Insurance?
A bonus (if declared) in life insurance is generally associated with participating or with-profit policies which means the insurer may declare bonuses from surplus profits earned during a financial year. The type of bonus also matters, since reversionary bonuses (if declared) are added to the sum assured while terminal bonuses (if declared) are paid only at maturity or claim settlement. In certain cases, such as compound reversionary bonuses, previously declared bonuses are added to the base sum assured, which further increases the amount of future bonuses.
Regular premium payment is equally important because only active policies remain eligible for bonuses (if declared). Policyholders should also carefully review their plan’s terms and conditions to understand the eligibility criteria. Consulting an insurer’s representative or using a bonus calculator can help estimate the potential corpus growth from bonuses.
Key Takeaways
● A bonus (if declared) in life insurance is declared on participating policies from the surplus profits that the insurer earns.
● Common types include reversionary and terminal bonuses, which can help enhance the corpus.
● Paying the premium regularly is essential for bonus eligibility.
● Bonus amounts may depend on policy terms, asset performance, and the surplus earned by the insurer.
● You can check the policy documents to understand the types of bonuses your policy might be eligible for.
Conclusion
Understand how a life insurance policy bonus works. The bonus (if declared) can add to your policy benefits and help you plan for your financial goals effectively. While bonus rates may vary each year, bonuses are added only to active participating policies. Therefore, paying premiums on time and keeping the policy in force is essential to remain eligible for future bonus additions. However, before you decide, review each step of buying life insurance, examine your future goals, and choose a suitable plan offering bonus addition that match your financial needs.
FAQs
1. Does the premium increase if a bonus has been declared the previous year?
Your premium is unlikely to be affected by the bonuses declared by the insurance company. Other factors, such as an increase in age, change in lifestyle and health issues, may have an impact on the premium. One can use the life insurance premium calculator to get an estimate of their premium amount based on different factors.
2. Are bonuses declared every year?
Whether bonuses are declared every year or not may depend on multiple factors like type of plan, insurance company’s performance, market performance, etc. It may prove to be helpful to consider the consistency of bonuses and the rate of bonuses when choosing an insurance company or policy. One should also keep in mind that past performance in terms of bonuses declared by life insurance companies may not be indicative of future bonus declaration.
3. What is the difference between a simple reversionary bonus and a compound reversionary bonus? A simple bonus applies only to the sum assured, while a compound bonus includes previously declared amounts, potentially increasing the overall policy benefits over time.
4. Are life insurance bonuses guaranteed for policyholders? No, bonuses are not guaranteed , the insurer may declare bonuses from surplus profits earned during a financial year . To remain eligible for future bonuses, the policy must stay in force—this requires paying premiums on time so the policy does not lapse or become paid-up.
5. Can I withdraw the bonus before maturity? Generally, bonuses (if declared) are payable at maturity or claim settlement. They cannot be withdrawn early. However, under policies offering cash bonus, the bonus earned can be paid to the policyholder in the same financial year it is delcared. Such bonuses can be used or withdrawn before maturity.
6. Do all insurers declare the same type of bonuses? No, bonus types vary by insurer and plan. The bonus rates depend on the surplus that the insurer earns.
